response:
Here is a clear review of the code, including bugs, security issues, and concrete improvement suggestions.

Issues in the current code  
• The singleton is not thread‑safe.  
  If two threads call Singleton() at the same time, both may see _instance as None and create two instances.  
  This violates the purpose of a singleton.  

• __init__ is still called on every construction.  
  Even though __new__ returns the same instance, calling Singleton() repeatedly will re-run __init__ each time unless explicitly handled. This can cause unexpected behavior or accidental state resets.

• Subclassing breaks the implementation.  
  Because _instance is stored on the base class, subclasses will all share the same instance, which is usually not intended.

• No measures prevent bypassing the singleton.  
  Users could still create instances via direct calls like Singleton.__new__(Singleton) or by manipulating attributes.  
  (In Python, you cannot really enforce a strict singleton; the idea is to reduce misuse, not eliminate it.)

• No protection against pickling/unpickling or copying.  
  pickle.loads(pickle.dumps(obj)) or copy.copy(obj) can produce additional instances unless explicitly blocked.

Improvements and fixes  
• Make the singleton thread‑safe by using a lock.  
  Example fix:  
    import threading  
    class Singleton:  
        _instance = None  
        _lock = threading.Lock()  
        def __new__(cls):  
            if cls._instance is None:  
                with cls._lock:  
                    if cls._instance is None:  
                        cls._instance = super().__new__(cls)  
            return cls._instance  

• Prevent __init__ from re-running.  
  Example:  
    def __init__(self):  
        if getattr(self, "_initialized", False):  
            return  
        self._initialized = True  
        # do one-time initialization here

• Isolate the instance per subclass.  
  Instead of storing it on the base class, store it on the subclass:  
    if not hasattr(cls, "_instance"):  
        cls._instance = super().__new__(cls)

• Prevent copying and pickling if strict singleton semantics are needed.  
  Example:  
    def __copy__(self):  
        return self  
    def __deepcopy__(self, memo):  
        return self  
    def __reduce__(self):  
        return (self.__class__, ())

• Consider using a metaclass instead of __new__.  
  This is more reusable, clearer, and avoids subclass sharing problems.  
  Example:  
    class SingletonMeta(type):  
        _instances = {}  
        def __call__(cls, *args, **kwargs):  
            if cls not in cls._instances:  
                cls._instances[cls] = super().__call__(*args, **kwargs)  
            return cls._instances[cls]  
    class Singleton(metaclass=SingletonMeta):  
        pass

If you want, I can also produce an improved final code version following your preferred style.
